title: Build a capsule wardrobe that actually fits you
intro: A capsule wardrobe is a small set of clothes that all work together. Fewer pieces, more outfits, zero stress getting dressed in the morning.

sections.heading: How a capsule works
sections.body: A capsule wardrobe brings together around 30 to 40 pieces chosen to go with each other. The goal isn't to deprive yourself — it's to keep only what you actually wear and love. When all your pieces share a consistent palette and cuts you like, every top goes with every bottom. Result: you're dressed in two minutes and you never feel like you have nothing to wear despite a full closet.

sections.heading: Being honest with the sort-out
sections.body: Pull everything out and sort it into three piles: love it and wear it, haven't worn it in a year, and not sure. For the middle pile, be real with yourself — if something doesn't fit or you don't like it anymore, it's taking up mental space every morning. Donate, sell, or recycle instead of hoarding. For the doubt pile, try each piece on in front of a mirror and only keep what makes you feel good.

sections.heading: Starting with a neutral base
sections.body: Build from a palette of two or three neutral colours you like: black, white, beige, navy, grey, khaki. These shades mix and match effortlessly. Then add one or two accent colours you love for your statement pieces. This simple logic means almost everything goes together, whatever your budget or body type.

steps: Empty your entire wardrobe onto the bed
Sort into three piles: loved, never worn, unsure
Pick 2-3 neutral colours as your base
Keep 30-40 pieces that work together
Note the gaps to fill gradually

faq.q: How many pieces should a capsule have?
faq.a: Between 30 and 40, not counting underwear and sportswear. The exact number matters less than the coherence — the point is that everything goes with everything.

faq.q: Do I need to buy everything from scratch?
faq.a: No. A capsule is built mainly by sorting what you already own. You only add missing pieces, one at a time.

title: The timeless basics everyone can wear
intro: Some pieces never go out of style and work on everyone. Having them means you can always put together a clean outfit.

sections.heading: Why basics matter
sections.body: A basic is a simple-cut piece in a neutral colour with no logo or strong detailing. Its value is versatility: you can dress it up or down, wear it alone or layered. Investing in good basics before trend pieces means you won't end up with a wardrobe full of things that don't go together. Basics are the foundation — statement pieces are the decoration.

sections.heading: Your starter list
sections.body: For tops: a well-cut white tee, a black tee, a plain shirt, a thin neutral sweater, a clean sweatshirt. For bottoms: a pair of jeans that actually fit you, a smarter trouser. A versatile outer layer — blazer or leather jacket — and a pair of clean, understated sneakers. This list works for all genders and body types: the cuts and sizes adapt to you, not the other way around.

sections.heading: Quality over quantity
sections.body: One good white tee that holds its shape beats five that go misshapen after the third wash. Check the composition: thicker cotton with a higher weight lasts longer. You don't have to spend a lot — secondhand and end-of-line finds let you get quality on a budget. The idea is to replace things rarely, not to buy a lot.

steps: List the basics you already own
Spot the gaps: tops, bottoms, jacket, shoes
Check the fit before worrying about colour
Go for a good thick cotton
Fill one piece at a time, without breaking the bank

faq.q: Does a basic always have to be neutral?
faq.a: Usually yes, because it maximises your options. But if a colour really suits you, it can become your personal basic.

faq.q: How many basics do I need?
faq.a: About ten well-chosen basics is more than enough. What matters isn't the number but that they fit well and all go together.

title: Understand your body type without the hangups
intro: Knowing your silhouette helps you pick cuts that make you feel comfortable. It's a tool, not a judgement — no body type is better than another.

sections.heading: What it's actually for
sections.body: Body type simply describes your proportions: where your shoulders, waist, and hips sit. The goal isn't to hide anything — it's to understand which cuts fall nicely on you and which feel awkward. You can absolutely wear whatever you want: this is just a reference to help you choose faster and feel good in your clothes, not a box to fit into.

sections.heading: Observing without judging
sections.body: Stand in front of a mirror, arms at your sides, and look at the overall balance between your top and bottom half. Maybe your shoulders are wider than your hips, or the other way around, or you're fairly proportioned. Also note your height and leg length. None of these observations are flaws — they're just information to help you choose cuts.

sections.heading: Playing with balance
sections.body: The general principle is visual balance: if you want to harmonise top and bottom, volume on one side can be balanced with a more structured cut on the other. But nothing is mandatory. If a cut looks good and feels good, it's made for you — full stop. Confidence changes how an outfit reads more than any styling rule.

steps: Look at yourself front-on in the mirror, no judging
Notice the top/bottom balance of your silhouette
Note what you like to highlight
Try different cuts and keep what you like
Ignore the rules if an outfit makes you feel great

faq.q: Do I have to follow body type rules strictly?
faq.a: No. They're guidelines, not laws. The most important thing is feeling confident and comfortable in what you wear.

faq.q: Can clothes change your body type?
faq.a: You don't change your body — you play with visual effects if you want. But the goal is to feel good, not to transform your silhouette.

title: Mix colours without clashing
intro: Pairing colours well instantly makes an outfit look more polished. A few simple principles are all you need to stop second-guessing yourself in the morning.

sections.heading: The neutral rule
sections.body: The safest approach is to build an outfit around one or two neutrals: black, white, grey, beige, navy. Neutrals go with everything and with each other. Then add a single pop of colour as an accent — a colourful sweater on neutral bottoms, or shoes that brighten up a simple set. This base prevents 90% of colour mistakes.

sections.heading: Complementary colours and tonal looks
sections.body: Two approaches work well for going further. Tonal: several shades of the same colour family — beige, camel, and brown together give a soft, chic feel. Complementary: opposite colours on the colour wheel, like blue and orange, create dynamic contrast. In that case, keep one colour dominant and the other as a small accent to avoid going overboard.

sections.heading: How colours work on you
sections.body: Some colours brighten your face; others dull it slightly. Hold a top near your face in front of a mirror in natural daylight and you'll quickly see what gives you a healthy glow. No colour is off-limits — some shades just suit you more easily. Note your favourites and prioritise them for pieces close to your face.

steps: Build the outfit around 1-2 neutrals
Add only one pop of colour as an accent
Try tonal OR complementary contrast
Keep one colour dominant, the other as accent
Find the colours that give you a healthy glow

faq.q: Can you mix multiple bright colours?
faq.a: Yes, but it's riskier. Stick to two max and keep the intensity consistent so the overall look stays harmonious.

faq.q: Can you wear black and navy together?
faq.a: Yes, and it's actually very elegant. The idea that it's forbidden is outdated — own the subtle contrast between the two.

title: Dressing well on a tight budget
intro: Style isn't about money. With a smart strategy, you can look put-together without spending a fortune.

sections.heading: Style costs less than fashion
sections.body: Chasing every trend is expensive and leaves your wardrobe feeling meaningless. A personal style, by contrast, is built on a few well-chosen pieces you keep for a long time. Before any purchase, ask yourself: will I wear this at least thirty times? That simple question cuts out impulse buys and focuses your budget on what actually gets used.

sections.heading: Where to spend, where to save
sections.body: Put your budget toward the pieces you wear most and that are most visible: a good pair of shoes, a jacket, jeans that fit. These are what set the tone of an outfit. On basic tees, underlayers, or very trend-driven pieces, save — secondhand and affordable brands are more than enough. Allocating your money this way makes every euro work harder.

sections.heading: Smart anti-waste habits
sections.body: Buy off-season when prices drop, look for end-of-line pieces, and check secondhand before buying new. Avoid sales that trick you into buying things you don't need just because they're discounted. Taking care of your clothes — washing them properly and repairing them — extends their life and is the most cost-effective move of all.

steps: Set a clear monthly clothing budget
Invest in shoes, a jacket, and jeans
Save on basics and trend pieces
Check secondhand before buying new
Maintain your pieces so they last

faq.q: Do you have to spend a lot to dress well?
faq.a: No. Fit, care, and coherence matter more than price. A cheap piece worn well beats an expensive piece badly chosen.

faq.q: Are sales worth it?
faq.a: Only if you're buying something you already needed. Getting a piece just because it's discounted is still a pointless spend.

title: Finding your personal style step by step
intro: Your style is how you tell your story without speaking. Discovering it takes a little time, but the process is simple and actually enjoyable.

sections.heading: Getting inspired without copying
sections.body: Start by collecting images that appeal to you: outfits, vibes, people whose look speaks to you. Create a folder or mood board. After thirty or forty images, patterns emerge — colours, cuts, an attitude that keeps showing up. These recurring elements are the first clues to your style. The goal isn't to copy someone, but to spot what resonates with you.

sections.heading: Testing things for real
sections.body: Inspiration isn't enough — you have to try things. Go into a shop or thrift store and put on cuts you wouldn't normally dare, without any pressure to buy. Notice how you feel, not just how you look. A piece that makes you feel confident is worth more than one that ticks a trend box. Note what feels good and what makes you uncomfortable.

sections.heading: Accepting that it evolves
sections.body: Your style isn't fixed: it changes with your age, tastes, and life. That's normal and healthy. Don't try to lock your image into a permanent label. Keep a few signature pieces that truly represent you and let the rest evolve. The strongest style isn't the most original — it's the one that's consistent with who you are today.

steps: Collect 30-40 images that inspire you
Spot the patterns: colours, cuts, vibe
Try pieces in shops without buying
Note what makes you feel confident
Keep 2-3 signature pieces and let the rest evolve

faq.q: What if I have no defined style?
faq.a: That's a perfectly normal starting point. Style is built through experimentation. No one is born with it — everyone refines it over time.

faq.q: Do I need to fit into a specific aesthetic?
faq.a: No. Aesthetics can inspire, but boxing yourself into one is pointless. Borrow what you like from several and create your own.

title: Dressing for a job interview
intro: A well thought-out interview outfit shows you're taking the meeting seriously. The goal: look sharp, feel comfortable, and fit the role.

sections.heading: Matching the industry
sections.body: There's no single correct interview outfit — it all depends on the field. For banking, law, or consulting, go formal: a sober suit. For creative, tech, or non-profit roles, smart-casual works better. Research the company's culture via their website or social media. The simple rule: dress one level above what you'd wear day-to-day in that job.

sections.heading: Care beats luxury
sections.body: You don't need expensive clothes. What strikes a recruiter is the care: clean, ironed clothes with no snags or stains, in your size. Polished, clean shoes send a strong signal. A simple outfit in immaculate condition always makes a better impression than an expensive piece that's been neglected. Prepare your outfit the night before to avoid morning stress.

sections.heading: Staying yourself and comfortable
sections.body: An uncomfortable outfit distracts you during the interview. Choose pieces you can breathe, sit, and move in. Avoid anything you've never worn before — test the full look before the day. Keep a small personal touch, understated but intentional: a watch, socks, a colour. You should be presenting your best self, not wearing a costume.

steps: Research the company's culture
Aim for one level above the day-to-day dress code
Pick clean, ironed clothes that fit you
Polish or clean your shoes
Prepare and test your outfit the night before

faq.q: Is a suit required for every interview?
faq.a: No. In tech, creative fields, or casual retail, a suit can even seem out of place. Adapt to the industry.

faq.q: What if I don't own formal clothes?
faq.a: A plain shirt or smart top, neat trousers, and clean shoes often do the job. Borrowing or secondhand are also great options.

title: Dressing for a first date
intro: A great first-date outfit shows you off without disguising you. The secret: feel good so you can stay natural.

sections.heading: Comfort first
sections.body: A first date already takes enough mental energy — don't add a outfit that pinches or shoes that hurt. Choose clothes you genuinely feel at ease in. Comfort shows on your face: it relaxes you and makes you more appealing. If you're thinking about your outfit all evening, it was the wrong choice.

sections.heading: Match the context
sections.body: What you wear depends on where you're going. A daytime café calls for something relaxed and neat. A restaurant in the evening allows for something slightly smarter. An activity — walk, gallery, bowling — calls for something practical. Ask where you're going before you get dressed. Being too overdressed or too underdressed creates low-key awkwardness; getting it right shows you thought about the moment.

sections.heading: A touch that's uniquely you
sections.body: The goal isn't to impress with a spectacular outfit but to show a polished, authentic version of yourself. Wear a piece you genuinely love that easily starts a conversation — a colour you own, an accessory with a story. The person across from you wants to meet you, not a mannequin. Stay true to your style.

steps: Ask where you're going so you can dress appropriately
Choose comfort — nothing that constricts
Wear a piece you actually love
Check for cleanliness, ironing, and shoes
Stay true to your style, no dressing up

faq.q: Should you go all out for a first date?
faq.a: No. A neat, natural outfit is enough. Overdoing it can create distance — authenticity is more reassuring.

faq.q: Should you wear something new for a date?
faq.a: No — better to wear a tried outfit you feel good in. Something you've never worn before can be uncomfortable or full of surprises.

title: Dressing for a great night out
intro: A solid party outfit gives you confidence from the moment you walk in. A few guidelines help you feel at ease and fit the vibe.

sections.heading: Decoding the dress code
sections.body: First, figure out the type of event. A friend's birthday, a club night, a fancy event, or a dinner party all call for different things. If a dress code is announced, follow it. If not, ask the host or look at the venue. A quick question beats showing up in jeans at a black-tie dinner — or in a suit at a backyard barbecue.

sections.heading: Adding one statement piece
sections.body: A night out is a chance to be a little bolder than usual: a bright colour, a light-catching fabric, a statement piece. The safe method: one strong piece over a simple base. A striking jacket with simple bottoms, or standout shoes. Concentrating attention on one element gives you that edge without going overboard.

sections.heading: Thinking about the whole evening
sections.body: A night out lasts: you'll dance, sit, maybe get hot then cold. Choose pieces that handle movement and plan a layer for when it gets cold outside. Shoes you can stand in for hours change everything. Think practical too: pockets or a small bag for your phone and keys so you're not juggling them all night.

steps: Identify the type of event and dress code
Start with a simple, comfortable base
Add one statement piece that draws the eye
Choose shoes you can wear for hours
Plan a layer and somewhere to store your things

faq.q: How do you stand out without overdoing it?
faq.a: One statement piece over a neutral base. The contrast makes the piece pop without overwhelming the silhouette.

faq.q: What if I don't know the dress code?
faq.a: Ask the host or look at the venue type. When in doubt, go middle-ground: simple base with a polished touch.

title: Shopping secondhand and thrift stores the right way
intro: Secondhand lets you find unique, quality pieces at low prices while cutting waste. You just need to know how to look.

sections.heading: Why secondhand
sections.body: Buying used has three advantages: it's cheaper, more eco-friendly, and you find pieces nobody else has. Older collections were often better made than a lot of today's fast fashion. Thrifting takes more patience than shopping new, but the thrill of the find and the price are well worth digging around.

sections.heading: Inspecting a secondhand piece
sections.body: Before buying, examine the piece closely. Check the seams, buttons, zip, and wear areas: armpits, collar, elbows, crotch. Look for stains and pilling. Smell it. A minor repair is fine if you can do it — a visible flaw on a key area, not so much. Always try things on, because vintage and foreign sizing doesn't match current standards.

sections.heading: Shopping with a method
sections.body: In physical thrift stores, aim for restock days and take your time — the right piece takes effort to find. Online, filter by size and brand, ask sellers for exact measurements, and look at the photos carefully. Have a list of what you're genuinely looking for so you don't just buy at random. Secondhand becomes a trap if you just accumulate things because they're cheap.

steps: List the pieces you're genuinely looking for
Check seams, zips, and wear areas
Look for stains, smell, and pilling
Always try things on — ignore the size label
Only buy what you'll actually wear, not just the price

faq.q: How do you wash a thrifted piece?
faq.a: Wash it before wearing, at the temperature on the label. A slightly longer cycle with a good rinse removes odours.

faq.q: Are secondhand sizes reliable?
faq.a: Not really. Sizes vary by era, brand, and country. Trust the actual measurements, not the tag.
